title: Design a Scalable User Feed System

transcript.text: Design a Scalable User Feed System like Twitter or Facebook

blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the two or three hardest parts — the fan-out problem, the read hot-path, and consistency — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in a feed system is a brutal read/write asymmetry: writes are rare (a person posts a few times a day) but reads are constant (everyone refreshes constantly), and every write potentially needs to reach millions of followers. Almost every hard decision below traces back to "do we do the work when someone writes, or when someone reads?"

🏢 I'll target an **Amazon SDE-3 bar** — so I'll make cost and operations first-class and work backwards from the reader's experience. Tell me if you'd rather a Meta/Google tilt and I'll re-emphasize.

### Functional (the core flows — "above the line")

**F1 — Create a post (text + optional media).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "a user types something, maybe attaches a photo, hits Post, and it's saved."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: the post is small and cheap to store, but the moment it's saved we owe it to potentially millions of followers — the write triggers a fan-out, not just an insert.

**F2 — A post becomes visible to all followers of the author.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "everyone who follows me should be able to see what I just posted, soon."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: a celebrity with 50M followers means one post = 50M feed updates. Doing that synchronously would make posting take minutes; doing it lazily makes reads expensive. This is the fan-out problem.

**F3 — Get my home feed: most recent posts from accounts I follow, newest first.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "when I open the app, I see the latest posts from people I follow, freshest on top."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: naively this is "find everyone I follow, fetch their recent posts, merge-sort by time" — for a user following thousands of accounts that's thousands of queries per feed open, at millions of opens per second.

**F4 — Paginate the feed (page size + continuation token).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "I scroll, and more posts load — and it remembers where I was so I don't see repeats."
- ⚡ Why it's hard: the feed is changing under the user as new posts arrive, so naive offset pagination skips or duplicates posts. We need a stable cursor.

**Below the line (out of scope for the core):** likes/comments (mentioned as optional — I'll note where they'd attach), ranking/ML relevance (we do pure reverse-chronological), search, notifications, direct messages, ads, trending. I'll design the feed so ranking can be layered on later.

### Non-Functional

**N1 — Low read latency: p99 feed load under ~200ms.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "when you pull to refresh, the feed shows up in the blink of an eye, 99 times out of 100."
- 💥 What breaks without it: a 2-second feed feels broken; engagement and ad revenue fall off a cliff — feed latency is the product.

**N2 — High availability: ~99.99% on the read path (four nines).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "the feed is up; total downtime is under about an hour per year."
- 💥 What breaks without it: if reads fail, the app is a blank screen for everyone at once — the most visible possible outage.

**N3 — Horizontal scalability for read and write spikes.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "when a huge event makes everyone post and refresh at once, we add machines, not pray."
- 💥 What breaks without it: a Super Bowl moment or a celebrity event takes the whole site down instead of just costing more.

**N4 — Durability: no posted data is lost, even on node failure.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "once we say 'Posted,' your post never disappears, even if a server dies a second later."
- 💥 What breaks without it: users lose trust instantly — a social network that drops posts is dead.

**N5 — Eventual consistency on feeds is acceptable (a few seconds of delay is fine).**
- 🗣️ Plain words: "it's okay if your followers see your post a couple seconds late — but not minutes late."
- 💥 What breaks without it: if we demanded strong consistency, every post would have to synchronously update millions of feeds before returning — posting would be unusably slow.

> 💬 "The whole game here is the read/write asymmetry — reads dwarf writes, and one write can owe millions of reads. So I'll spend my design budget making reads cheap and pushing work onto the write path where I can."

🎙️ Script: "So at the core, three flows define this system: you create a post, that post fans out to your followers, and you read a merged, time-ordered feed of the people you follow — with pagination so scrolling works. The non-functionals that actually shape the design are low read latency and high availability on the read path, because the feed is the product, plus durability so we never drop a post. The one freedom I'm given is eventual consistency — followers seeing a post a few seconds late is fine — and I'm going to lean on that freedom hard, because it's what lets me do the expensive fan-out work asynchronously instead of blocking the writer."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "What's the scale — roughly how many daily active users, and how many posts per day?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "the entire design pivots on scale. At a few million users a single beefy database is fine; at hundreds of millions I have to shard and pre-compute feeds."
- ↔️ Design fork: If LARGE (100M+ DAU) → fan-out-on-write with pre-computed feeds in a cache. If SMALL → fan-out-on-read, just query on demand, skip the machinery.
- Assumption: I'll assume ~200M DAU, ~500M posts/day — large scale, so pre-computation is on the table.

**Q2 — "What's the follower distribution? Are there celebrity accounts with tens of millions of followers?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "if everyone follows a few hundred people it's uniform and easy; a handful of accounts with 50M followers is the single hardest thing in this design — one post becomes 50M writes."
- ↔️ Design fork: If celebrities exist → I need a hybrid (pull celebrity posts at read time, push everyone else). If the graph is flat → pure fan-out-on-write works.
- Assumption: yes, heavy-tailed with celebrities — so I'll design the hybrid from the start.

**Q3 — "Is the feed strictly reverse-chronological, or ranked by relevance?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "chronological is a merge-sort by timestamp; ranked means an ML scoring layer, a totally different read path."
- ↔️ Design fork: If chronological → store feed as a time-sorted list. If ranked → store candidates and score at read time with a feature store.
- Assumption: reverse-chronological for the core, designed so ranking can bolt on later.

**Q4 — "How fresh must the feed be — is a few seconds of delay between a post and it appearing acceptable?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "this decides whether I can do fan-out asynchronously. Seconds of slack lets me use a queue; sub-second would force synchronous writes."
- ↔️ Design fork: If seconds OK → async fan-out via a message queue. If real-time required → much harder, synchronous push.
- Assumption: a few seconds is fine (matches N5).

**Q5 — "How big can a post be — what's the media story? Are we storing images/video ourselves?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "text is tiny; media is 1000x bigger and changes the storage and CDN bill entirely."
- ↔️ Design fork: If media → blob store (S3) + CDN, and the feed stores only URLs. If text-only → skip all of that.
- Assumption: media allowed; we store blobs in object storage and serve via CDN, feed carries only metadata + URLs.

**Q6 — "Read/write ratio — am I right that reads massively outnumber writes?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "it confirms where to spend the budget. A 100:1 read:write ratio means optimizing reads is everything."
- ↔️ Design fork: If read-heavy → pre-compute and cache feeds. If write-heavy (unusual) → optimize ingestion.
- Assumption: ~100:1 read:write — heavily read-dominated.

**Q7 — "Do we need likes/comments counts in the feed for v1?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: "counters are a separate high-write-volume problem; I want to know if it's in scope now or later."
- ↔️ Design fork: If YES → add a counter service (Redis counters + async aggregation). If NO → leave hooks and move on.
- Assumption: out of scope for core; I'll note the attachment point.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — 200M DAU, celebrities exist, chronological feed, eventual consistency, media via CDN. Before I size it, anything there you'd push back on?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I'll only compute the numbers that actually drive a decision. Glossing terms as I go: **DAU** = daily active users; **QPS** = queries per second (requests hitting servers each second); **TTL** = time-to-live, how long a cached value lives before expiring.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Write QPS (posts) | 500M posts/day ÷ 86,400 s/day ≈ 5,787/s; peak ≈ 2x average | ~12K/s | "around 12 thousand posts a second at peak" | Writes are modest — a sharded DB handles this easily. Writes are NOT the bottleneck. |
| Read QPS (feed loads) | 200M DAU × ~20 feed opens/day = 4B/day ÷ 86,400 ≈ 46K/s; peak ≈ 2x | ~90K/s | "roughly 90 thousand feed reads a second at peak" | Reads dominate by ~7-15x. Feeds MUST be served from cache, not computed live. |
| Fan-out write amplification | 12K posts/s × ~500 avg followers = 6M feed-inserts/s | ~6M/s | "about 6 million feed writes a second" | This is the real write load. It forces async fan-out via a queue + sharded feed cache, never synchronous. |
| Celebrity blow-up | 1 post × 50M followers = 50M inserts from a SINGLE post | 50M | "fifty million writes from one tweet" | THE flip-point: pure push is impossible for celebrities → hybrid (pull celeb posts at read time). |
| Feed cache storage | 200M users × ~800 post-ids cached × ~16 bytes (id+ts) ≈ 2.5 TB; ×3 replication ≈ 8 TB | ~8 TB | "single-digit terabytes of feed cache" | Fits comfortably across a Redis cluster (~40-80 nodes). Cache size is not the constraint; throughput is. |
| Post storage (5 yr) | 500M/day × 365 × 5 × ~500 bytes/post ≈ 450 TB; media separate in S3 | ~0.5 PB | "about half a petabyte of post text, media on top in S3" | Storage is a non-issue dollar-wise; the dominant cost is CDN egress for media + cache RAM. |

The ONE number that forces the design: **6M feed-writes/s from fan-out, spiking to 50M from a single celebrity post.** A single Redis node tops out around ~100-150K ops/s, so 6M/s means we shard the feed cache across dozens of nodes — and the 50M-from-one-post case can't be pushed at all, which is exactly why celebrities get pulled at read time instead.

🎙️ Script: "The headline numbers: about 12 thousand posts a second, but around 90 thousand feed reads a second — reads win by roughly ten to one. And because each post fans out to hundreds of followers, the real write load is about 6 million feed updates a second, which already says 'async queue, sharded cache.' But the number that actually breaks the naive design is the celebrity: one post to 50 million followers is 50 million writes, which you simply can't push in real time — so I'll spend my budget making reads cheap with pre-computed feeds, and treat celebrities as a special case I pull instead of push."

## 4. Core Entities

This is a first draft of the entities — I'll add full field definitions in §7.

- **User** — a person with a profile; owns their identity, their posts, and their follow relationships.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "think of it like a contact card — it's who you are and the anchor everything else hangs off of."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "how do you look up someone's followers fast?" → the Follow edges are indexed both directions (followers-of and following-of), so both lookups are O(1) index hits.

- **Post** — a single piece of content (text + optional media URL + author + timestamp); immutable once created.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "like a sticky note with a time written on it — it never changes after you write it, which is what lets us cache it forever."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "why is a post immutable?" → immutability means we can cache it aggressively with no invalidation logic — edits become a new version, not a mutation.

- **Follow (edge)** — a directed relationship: follower → followee. The social graph.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "like a one-way subscription — I follow you doesn't mean you follow me."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "how do you fan out a post?" → you query 'who follows the author' (the followers list) and push the post id into each of their feeds.

- **Feed (timeline)** — a per-user, time-ordered list of post-ids the user should see; this is the pre-computed read-optimized view.
  - 🗣️ Simple analogy: "think of it like a personal inbox that's already sorted newest-first — when you open the app we just read the top of your inbox, no assembling required."
  - ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "is this the source of truth?" → no, it's a derived cache; if it's lost we rebuild it from Posts + Follows. That's what makes it safe to keep in Redis.

🎙️ Script: "There are really four entities. Users and Posts are obvious. The Follow edge is the social graph — directed, so I index it both ways so I can ask both 'who do you follow' and 'who follows you' cheaply. The one that's the heart of the design is the Feed — a per-user, pre-sorted list of post-ids that I treat as a derived cache, not a source of truth. That distinction matters: because the feed is rebuildable from posts and follows, I can keep it in Redis and not panic if a node dies. I'll give full field definitions in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS; the caller is always identified from a JWT (**JWT** = a signed token the client carries that proves who they are — we never trust a user id sent in the body).

| Method + Path | Auth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| POST /v1/posts | JWT | Create a post (F1) |
| GET /v1/feed | JWT | Get home feed, paginated (F3, F4) |
| POST /v1/follows | JWT | Follow a user (supports F2 fan-out targeting) |
| DELETE /v1/follows/{userId} | JWT | Unfollow |

**POST /v1/posts** — POST because it creates new server state.
- Key request fields: `text: string` (the body), `mediaIds: string[]` (ids of already-uploaded blobs — client uploads media to S3 first via a pre-signed URL, then references ids, so we never funnel image bytes through the API). `Idempotency-Key: header` — a client-generated unique id so a retried request after a network blip doesn't create a duplicate post.
- Intentionally absent: `authorId` (taken from the JWT, never the body — otherwise anyone could post as anyone) and `createdAt` (server-stamped, so timestamps can't be forged to game ordering).
```
// Request
POST /v1/posts
Idempotency-Key: 9f2a-cli-001
{ "text": "shipping the new feed!", "mediaIds": ["med_abc123"] }

// Response 201
{ "postId": "post_def456", "authorId": "u_777", "createdAt": "2026-06-19T14:32:00Z", "status": "ACCEPTED" }
```
- Key design decision: returns `ACCEPTED` immediately after durably writing the post — fan-out happens asynchronously. The writer doesn't wait for millions of feed updates.
- 🗣️ Narration: "Posting returns the instant the post is safely stored; the work of spraying it to followers happens in the background. The author id comes from the token, not the request, and an idempotency key kills duplicates from retries."

**GET /v1/feed** — GET because it only reads.
- Query params: `limit: int` (page size, default 20) and `cursor: string` (an opaque continuation token encoding the timestamp+id of the last seen post). We use a cursor, NOT an offset, because the feed shifts as new posts arrive — offset would skip or repeat posts.
```
// Request
GET /v1/feed?limit=20&cursor=eyJ0cyI6MTcxOC...

// Response 200
{ "posts": [ { "postId": "post_999", "authorId": "u_12", "text": "...", "mediaUrls": ["https://cdn/.../x.jpg"], "createdAt": "2026-06-19T14:31:50Z" } ],
  "nextCursor": "eyJ0cyI6MTcxOC4uLn0" }
```
- Key design decision: cursor-based (keyset) pagination gives stable scrolling under a moving feed; `nextCursor` is null when the feed end is reached.
- 🗣️ Narration: "Reading the feed is a GET with a limit and a cursor. The cursor is just an encoded 'where I left off' marker — that's what makes infinite scroll stable even as new posts pour in at the top."

**POST /v1/follows** — POST creates a new edge.
- Key fields: `followeeId: string` (who to follow); follower is from the JWT. This edge is what the fan-out service reads to know where to deliver future posts.
- 🗣️ Narration: "Following is just creating a directed edge; the interesting part is downstream — this list is exactly what the fan-out worker walks when this person posts."

> 💬 "The security rule I'd say out loud: identity always comes from the token, and timestamps and author ids are server-set — the client can never forge who posted or when."

🎙️ Script: "Four endpoints. Posting is a POST that returns immediately once the post is durable, with an idempotency key to dedupe retries and the author taken from the token. Reading the feed is a GET with a limit and an opaque cursor for stable pagination. Following creates a directed edge that the fan-out path later reads. The throughline is that the server never trusts client-supplied identity or timestamps — those come from the JWT and the server clock."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Create a post"

The user writes a post; we must store it durably and acknowledge fast, before any fan-out happens.

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| API Gateway | The front door that authenticates, rate-limits, and routes every request | Need one place to verify JWTs and throttle abuse before traffic hits services | Not direct-to-service (no central auth/rate-limit, repeated everywhere); not a monolith front controller (doesn't scale independently) | One more network hop (~1-2ms) |
| Post Service | A stateless service that validates and writes posts | Posting needs validation + a durable write + emitting a fan-out event — a clear bounded responsibility | Not folding it into a monolith (can't scale post-writes independently of reads) | More services to operate |
| Post DB (Cassandra) | A wide-column store that spreads rows across many nodes and is built for high write throughput | We need ~12K durable writes/s, sharded by post id, with no single write bottleneck | Not a single Postgres (one primary caps write throughput, becomes the bottleneck) | Eventual consistency + no rich joins (fine — posts are looked up by id) |
| Message Queue (Kafka) | A durable append-only log that buffers events so nothing is lost if consumers lag | Decouples the fast ack from the slow fan-out; absorbs spikes | Not synchronous fan-out (would make posting take seconds); not in-memory queue (loses events on crash) | A few seconds of fan-out lag (acceptable per N5) |

> **API Gateway** — the single entry point that checks your token and rate-limits you. Here it means auth and abuse control live in one place, not scattered across services.
> **Cassandra** — a database that splits data across many machines by a key and is tuned for heavy writes. Here it stores posts so 12K writes/s never hit a single bottleneck.
> **Kafka** — a durable log that holds a firehose of events until downstream workers catch up. Here it holds "new post" events so the author gets an instant ack while fan-out happens behind the scenes.

**Create-post flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: POST /v1/posts"| GW[API Gateway]
    GW -->|"2: authenticated write"| PS[Post Service]
    PS -->|"3: durable insert"| PDB[(Post DB - Cassandra)]
    PS -->|"4: emit post-created"| MQ[[Kafka]]
    PS -->|"5: 201 ACCEPTED"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,PS svc
    class MQ queue
    class PDB db
```
↳ new components: API Gateway, Post Service, Post DB, Kafka.

Numbered narration:
1. Client sends POST /v1/posts with the JWT. The gateway authenticates and rate-limits — because we want one choke point for abuse before any service is touched. If the token's invalid, it's rejected here; nothing downstream sees it.
2. Gateway forwards to the Post Service, the only thing that owns post creation. State so far: nothing persisted yet.
3. Post Service writes the post to Cassandra, sharded by post id. After this step the post is durable — even if everything else crashes now, the post survives (satisfies N4). If the write fails, we return an error and the client retries with the same idempotency key.
4. Post Service emits a `post-created` event to Kafka. State change: the fan-out is now guaranteed to happen eventually because Kafka durably holds the event. If Kafka is briefly down, we retry; the post is already safe.
5. We return 201 ACCEPTED. The user sees "Posted" — they never wait for fan-out.

> 💬 "The key move: I make the post durable and acknowledge in milliseconds, then hand the expensive fan-out to Kafka — the writer never pays for their followers."

🎙️ Script: "When you post, the gateway authenticates you, the Post Service writes the post to Cassandra so it's durable, drops a 'new post' event onto Kafka, and immediately tells you it's accepted. Cassandra because we need heavy sharded writes with no single bottleneck; Kafka because it lets me decouple the instant acknowledgement from the slow fan-out and never lose an event. The trade-off is your followers see the post a couple seconds later — which we already agreed is fine."

### 6.2 "A post becomes visible to all followers" (fan-out)

This is the heart of the system: turning one post into entries in many followers' feeds — without melting on a celebrity.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fan-out Service | A pool of workers that consume post events and write the post-id into each follower's feed | Need to spread one post to hundreds of feeds asynchronously and scale workers with load | Not doing it inside Post Service (would block the writer); not a cron batch (too slow, feeds stale for minutes) | Complexity of a worker tier + at-least-once delivery to dedupe |
| Graph DB / Follow store | Stores follower lists; answers "who follows X" fast | Fan-out must read the author's followers in one cheap lookup | Not scanning a relational table (too slow at 50M rows); | Denormalized, kept in sync via events |
| Feed Cache (Redis) | An in-memory store holding each user's feed as a sorted list of post-ids | Reads must be served from RAM at 90K/s with p99 < 200ms | Not reading Cassandra per feed open (too slow, too many queries) | It's a cache — must be rebuildable; bounded to ~800 ids/user |

> **Fan-out Service** — background workers that take a new post and write it into each follower's feed list. Here it's what makes reads cheap by doing the work at write time.
> **Redis** — an in-memory key-value store that's blisteringly fast because data lives in RAM. Here it holds pre-computed feeds so a feed open is one fast read, not thousands of queries.

**Fan-out flow (hybrid push/pull)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MQ[[Kafka post-created]]
    MQ -->|"1: consume event"| FS[Fan-out Service]
    FS -->|"2: is author a celebrity?"| FS
    FS -->|"3a: normal - get followers"| GDB[(Follow store)]
    FS -->|"4a: push post-id to each feed"| RC[(Feed Cache - Redis)]
    FS -.->|"3b: celebrity - skip push"| CEL[(Celebrity Posts cache)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class MQ queue
    class FS svc
    class GDB,RC,CEL db
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1). New: Fan-out Service, Follow store, Feed Cache, Celebrity Posts cache.

Numbered narration:
1. A Fan-out worker pulls a `post-created` event from Kafka. Kafka gives at-least-once delivery, so workers must be idempotent (writing the same post-id to a feed twice is a no-op because the feed is a sorted set keyed by post-id).
2. The worker checks the author's follower count. WHY: this branch is the entire celebrity defense.
3a/4a. Normal author (< ~100K followers): read the follower list from the Follow store, and push the post-id into each follower's Redis feed (trimming to the newest ~800). State change: hundreds of feeds now contain this post. If a feed write fails, retry — at-least-once + idempotent writes make this safe.
3b. Celebrity author: do NOT push. Instead the post just lives in the author's own posts list (and a hot "celebrity posts" cache). State change: nothing is fanned out — read-time merge handles it (see 6.3). This is what stops one post from becoming 50M writes.

> 💬 "The one insight I'd point at: I push for normal users and pull for celebrities — that single branch is what keeps fan-out from exploding."

🎙️ Script: "A fan-out worker picks up the new-post event and asks one question: is this author a celebrity? For normal accounts it grabs the follower list and writes the post-id into each follower's Redis feed, trimmed to the most recent few hundred — so reads are basically free later. For a celebrity it does nothing at fan-out time, because pushing to 50 million feeds is impossible; their posts get merged in at read time instead. Kafka delivers at-least-once so the workers are written to be idempotent — re-writing a post-id to a feed is harmless."

### 6.3 "Get my home feed (newest first)"

The reader opens the app; we must return a merged, time-sorted page in under ~200ms.

| Component | What it is | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed Service | Stateless service that assembles a user's feed page | Need to merge pushed feed + pulled celebrity posts and hydrate post bodies | Not letting clients query DBs directly (security, coupling) | Extra hop, but enables caching + merge logic |
| Feed Cache (Redis) | (reused) pre-computed list of post-ids per user | Serves the bulk of the feed from RAM | — | — |
| Post Cache (Redis) | Caches post bodies by id | After getting ids, we hydrate bodies in one batched mget | Not reading Cassandra per post (slow) | Cache miss falls back to Cassandra |

**Read feed flow**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: GET /v1/feed"| GW[API Gateway]
    GW -->|"2: route"| FSV[Feed Service]
    FSV -->|"3: read post-ids"| RC[(Feed Cache - Redis)]
    FSV -->|"4: merge celebrity posts"| CEL[(Celebrity Posts cache)]
    FSV -->|"5: hydrate bodies mget"| PC[(Post Cache - Redis)]
    PC -.->|"6: miss fallback"| PDB[(Post DB - Cassandra)]
    FSV -->|"7: page + nextCursor"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,FSV svc
    class RC,CEL,PC,PDB db
```
↳ reuses existing: API Gateway, Feed Cache, Post DB (from §6.1–6.2). New: Feed Service, Post Cache.

Numbered narration:
1. Client sends GET /v1/feed with limit + cursor. Gateway authenticates.
2. Routed to the Feed Service, which owns assembly.
3. Feed Service reads the page of post-ids from the user's Redis feed list, starting at the cursor. This is one fast RAM read. State: we now have ~20 ids of pushed posts.
4. It fetches recent posts from the handful of celebrities this user follows (the pull half) and merges them by timestamp into the page. WHY: celebrities weren't pushed, so we add them here — that's the other side of the hybrid. If the celebrity cache is cold, we read their posts list from Cassandra.
5. With the merged id list, it batch-reads post bodies from the Post Cache in one `mget`. State: ids are now full posts (text + media URLs).
6. On a cache miss, fall back to Cassandra for those ids and backfill the cache. Worst case (cold cache) is slower but correct.
7. Return the page plus `nextCursor`. If any dependency is down, we degrade — serve a slightly stale feed rather than error (N2).

> 💬 "Reading is 'grab pre-sorted ids from RAM, sprinkle in the few celebrities I follow, hydrate bodies in one batch' — almost no real computation on the hot path."

🎙️ Script: "Opening the feed reads a page of pre-sorted post-ids straight from your Redis timeline, then merges in recent posts from the few celebrities you follow — that's the pull half of the hybrid. It hydrates the bodies with a single batched Redis read, falling back to Cassandra only on a miss, and returns the page with a cursor for the next one. Because the heavy lifting already happened at write time, the read is essentially a couple of in-memory lookups — which is how we hold p99 under 200 milliseconds at 90 thousand reads a second."

### 6.4 "Paginate the feed"

Pagination reuses the read path — the only new concept is the cursor, no new components.

↳ builds on §6.3 diagram — no new components.

The cursor encodes `(timestamp, postId)` of the last item returned, base64-ed into an opaque token. The Feed Service reads the next slice of the sorted feed list *strictly older than* that key (keyset pagination), so even as new posts arrive at the top, the user's scroll position is stable — they never see a duplicate or skip a post. When the list end is reached, `nextCursor` is null.

> 💬 "I use a cursor, not an offset, because the feed is a moving target — offset 40 means something different a second later; a keyset cursor is anchored to a real post."

🎙️ Script: "Pagination doesn't add components — it adds a cursor. Instead of 'give me rows 40 to 60,' which breaks when new posts shift everything down, the cursor says 'give me posts older than this exact timestamp and id.' That keyset approach keeps infinite scroll stable under a feed that's constantly growing at the top."

**Final (high-level)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[Client app]
    Client -->|"1: post / read"| GW[API Gateway]
    subgraph WritePath["Write / Fan-out Path"]
        GW -->|"2: write"| PS[Post Service]
        PS -->|"3: insert"| PDB[(Post DB - Cassandra)]
        PS -->|"4: event"| MQ[[Kafka]]
        MQ -->|"5: consume"| FS[Fan-out Service]
        FS -->|"6: followers"| GDB[(Follow store)]
        FS -->|"7: push ids"| RC[(Feed Cache - Redis)]
    end
    subgraph ReadPath["Read Path"]
        GW -->|"8: GET feed"| FSV[Feed Service]
        FSV -->|"9: read ids"| RC
        FSV -->|"10: celeb merge"| CEL[(Celebrity Posts)]
        FSV -->|"11: hydrate"| PC[(Post Cache)]
        PC -.->|"12: miss"| PDB
    end
    Media[Media via CDN + S3] -.->|"async upload"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#2a1a3a,stroke:#6B46C1,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class GW,PS,FS,FSV svc
    class MQ queue
    class PDB,GDB,RC,CEL,PC db
    class Media ext
```

Load-bearing schema notes: Feed Cache = `feed:{userId}` → sorted set of `postId` scored by timestamp, capped ~800. Post DB = `posts` partitioned by `postId`; `user_posts` partitioned by `authorId` sorted by time (for the celebrity pull). Follow store = `followers:{userId}` and `following:{userId}` adjacency lists.

🎙️ Script: "Two halves connected by Kafka. On the write side: post lands in Cassandra, an event goes to Kafka, and fan-out workers push the post-id into followers' Redis feeds — except celebrities, who are skipped. On the read side: the Feed Service reads pre-sorted ids from Redis, merges in the celebrities you follow, hydrates bodies from a post cache, and pages with a cursor. Media rides a separate path entirely — uploaded straight to S3 and served by CDN, with only URLs in the feed."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. The two richest places to go deep are the fan-out/celebrity problem and the read hot-path caching — which is more interesting to you, or should I start with the celebrity case since it's the hardest?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — Entity table**

| Entity | Key fields (type, PK/shard) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| User | userId (PK), handle, name, followerCount | Cassandra / managed SQL | userId | Strong on profile |
| Post | postId (PK), authorId, text, mediaIds[], createdAt | Cassandra | postId | Eventual (immutable) |
| UserPosts (by author) | authorId (partition), createdAt (cluster), postId | Cassandra | authorId | Eventual |
| Follow | followerId, followeeId | Graph store / Cassandra | followeeId for followers list | Eventual |
| Feed | userId (key) → sorted set of postId by ts | Redis | userId | Eventual (rebuildable) |

**PART B — Storage decision cards**

🗄️ **Cassandra — used for: Posts, UserPosts, Follows**
1. What it is: a wide-column database that splits data across many machines by a partition key and is tuned for very high write rates, trading away rich joins and strong consistency.
2. Why we picked it: our binding write load is ~12K posts/s plus the fan-out, and access is always by key (post by id, posts by author, followers by user) — no joins needed. Cassandra's masterless writes mean no single primary to bottleneck on.
3. What we considered instead: **single Postgres** — one primary caps write throughput and becomes the bottleneck under fan-out; **MongoDB** — works, but Cassandra's write path and linear scale-out are a better fit for an append-heavy, key-access workload; **MySQL sharded** — viable but we'd hand-roll the sharding Cassandra gives natively.
4. Trade-off we accept: eventual consistency and no cross-partition joins, so we denormalize (a separate UserPosts table) — fine because posts are immutable and read by key.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Posts and follows go in Cassandra because it's append-heavy, key-accessed, and needs to scale writes linearly with no single primary. I considered Postgres but one write primary would bottleneck under fan-out. The cost is no joins and eventual consistency, so I denormalize and that's fine because posts never change."

🗄️ **Redis — used for: Feed cache, Post body cache, Celebrity posts**
1. What it is: an in-memory key-value store that's extremely fast because everything lives in RAM, with native sorted-set and list types.
2. Why we picked it: feed reads must hit RAM to make p99 < 200ms at 90K/s, and the sorted-set type is exactly a time-ordered feed. 
3. What we considered instead: **Memcached** — no sorted sets, we'd rebuild ordering ourselves; **read from Cassandra directly** — too slow and too many queries per feed open; **local in-process cache** — can't share feeds across stateless service instances.
4. Trade-off we accept: it's volatile and bounded — we cap each feed at ~800 ids and treat it as rebuildable, not a source of truth.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Feeds live in Redis as sorted sets because reads have to come from memory and a sorted set is literally a time-ordered timeline. It's a cache, not truth — if a node dies I rebuild the feed from posts and follows. I bound each feed to a few hundred entries so memory stays predictable."

🗄️ **S3 + CDN — used for: media blobs**
1. What it is: object storage (S3) for large files, fronted by a CDN — a network of edge servers that cache content close to users.
2. Why we picked it: media is 1000x bigger than text; serving it from edge caches cuts latency and keeps bytes off our services.
3. What we considered instead: **storing blobs in Cassandra/DB** — bloats the DB and kills cache efficiency; **serving media from our own servers** — egress and latency disaster at scale.
4. Trade-off we accept: an extra upload step (client → pre-signed S3 URL) and CDN cost is the dominant bill.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Media goes straight to S3 and is served by a CDN; the feed only ever carries URLs. I'd never put image bytes in the database — it wrecks cache density and the CDN is far cheaper and faster for delivery."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Create post (durability) | Cassandra | Quorum write (strong-ish) | Must never lose a post (N4) |
| Fan-out feed write | Redis | Eventual | Seconds of lag fine (N5) |
| Read feed | Redis | Eventual | Slightly stale is acceptable |
| Read-your-own-writes | Feed Service | Strong for self | After posting, client injects own post into its local view / Feed Service reads author's own posts so you always see your post immediately |
| Follower count | Cassandra/counter | Eventual | Display-only, async aggregated |

🎙️ Script: "The dominant store is Cassandra for posts and the social graph — append-heavy, key-accessed, scales writes linearly, and I accept eventual consistency and no joins because posts are immutable. Redis holds the pre-computed feeds and post bodies because reads must come from RAM; I treat it strictly as a rebuildable cache. Media is S3 plus a CDN, with only URLs in the feed. I considered Postgres for the core but rejected it because a single write primary would bottleneck under fan-out. The one consistency subtlety is read-your-own-writes: I make sure you always see your own post immediately by merging your own recent posts at read time, even if fan-out hasn't finished."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 If you get stuck: go back to "reads dwarf writes, and one post can owe millions of reads" — every answer here flows from that.

### How do we handle fan-out (one post → millions of feeds)?

**#### Bad: Fan-out on read (pull everything live)**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: "when you open the app, go find everyone you follow and grab their latest posts right then, and merge them."
Approach: on each feed read, look up the user's followees, query each one's recent posts, merge-sort by time, return a page.
Why people try this: it's the obvious, correct, zero-precompute approach — writes are trivial.
⚠️ What breaks: a user following 2,000 accounts triggers ~2,000 queries per feed open; at 90K feed opens/s that's ~180M queries/s against the post store — completely impossible, and feed latency is hundreds of ms to seconds.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: read QPS × fan-in makes live merging un-serveable; we must pre-compute.

**#### Good: Fan-out on write (push to every follower)**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: reads were doing all the work live; we move the work to write time so reads become a single lookup.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: "the moment you post, we immediately drop it into the inbox of every one of your followers, so reading is just opening your inbox."
Approach: on `post-created`, the Fan-out Service reads the author's followers and writes the post-id into each follower's Redis feed (a sorted set, trimmed to ~800).
Why people try this: it makes the read path trivially fast — exactly what we want given the read:write ratio.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[New post] -->|"1: event"| W[Fan-out worker]
    W -->|"2: get followers"| G[(Follow store)]
    W -->|"3: write id to each feed"| R[(Redis feeds)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class P,W svc
    class G,R db
```
⚠️ What breaks: the celebrity. A 50M-follower account produces 50M feed writes from one post — a write storm that backs up Kafka, delays everyone's fan-out, and wastes huge effort on inactive followers.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: the heavy tail of the follower distribution — push doesn't work for the top accounts.

**#### Great: Hybrid push/pull (push normal, pull celebrities)**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it pushed for everyone, which explodes on celebrities; we stop pushing above a threshold and pull those at read time.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: "for normal accounts we still drop posts into followers' inboxes; for the handful of mega-accounts we don't — instead, when you open your feed, we go grab their latest posts and mix them in."
Approach: at fan-out, if the author's follower count > threshold (~100K), skip the push. At read time, the Feed Service merges the user's pushed feed with a live pull of the few celebrities they follow (cached in a hot "celebrity posts" list). Each user follows only a handful of celebrities, so the read-time merge is cheap.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Rd[Feed read] -->|"1: pushed ids"| RF[(Redis feed)]
    Rd -->|"2: pull celeb posts"| CP[(Celebrity posts cache)]
    Rd -->|"3: merge by time"| Out[Merged page]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class Rd,Out svc
    class RF,CP db
```
🔢 Decision-forcing math: pushing a celebrity = 50M writes/post; a Redis node does ~150K ops/s, so one celebrity post alone would saturate ~330 node-seconds. Pulling instead: each reader merges ~5-10 celebrities × 1 cached read = trivial. The threshold ~100K balances push cost vs. read-merge cost.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Worker crashes mid-fan-out | Some followers missing the post | At-least-once redelivery; idempotent feed writes (sorted set by id) |
| Duplicate event from Kafka | Same id written twice | Sorted set keyed by post-id → no-op |
| Celebrity cache cold | Read-time pull misses | Fall back to UserPosts in Cassandra, backfill cache |
| User crosses threshold (gains followers) | Mixed push/pull state | Backfill recent posts on transition; merge dedupes |

🎙️ Script: "Fan-out on read is correct but dies under the read load — thousands of queries per open at 90 thousand opens a second. So I push at write time into Redis feeds, which makes reads a single lookup. But pushing breaks on celebrities — 50 million writes from one post — so the real answer is hybrid: push for normal accounts, and for the handful of mega-accounts, skip the push and merge their posts in at read time. Since you only follow a few celebrities, that merge is cheap, and a follower-count threshold around 100K decides which path an account takes."
🧠 If they ask "what threshold and why?": "Around 100K followers — below it, pushing is cheaper than making every reader pull; above it, the write amplification dominates, so pulling wins. It's tunable per measured cost."

### How do we keep the read path fast and cheap (caching)?

**#### Good: Pre-computed feeds in Redis**
🗣️ Plain words: "keep each person's ready-made feed in fast memory so opening the app is one quick read."
Approach: feed = Redis sorted set of post-ids; reads pull a page directly. Post bodies cached separately by id.
Why people try this: RAM reads are the only way to hit sub-200ms at this QPS.
⚠️ What breaks: cache misses on post bodies and cold caches after a node failure cause latency spikes; a thundering herd can hit Cassandra when a hot post expires everywhere at once.
🔁 What forces upgrade: need to defend the miss path and hot keys.

**#### Great: Layered cache + hot-key defense**
↩️ What previous got wrong: a single cache layer with naive misses stampedes the DB. We add request-coalescing and hot-key replication.
🗣️ Plain words: "when lots of people want the same thing at once and it's not cached, only one request goes to the database and everyone shares the answer."
Approach: post bodies in Redis with TTL; on miss use single-flight (**single-flight** = only one concurrent request per key actually hits the DB, the rest wait for its result) to prevent stampedes; replicate hot posts (a viral post) across multiple cache shards / use local in-process LRU on Feed Service for the hottest few thousand posts.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: with a 95% post-cache hit ratio, 90K reads/s × ~20 posts/page × 5% miss ≈ 90K body fetches/s to Cassandra — sized across the cluster. Cold cache (0% hit) worst case ≈ 1.8M reads/s → we pre-warm and rate-limit rebuilds.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Redis node dies | A slice of feeds gone | Rebuild from Cassandra; replica promotion; reads degrade to slightly slower |
| Hot post expires everywhere | Stampede to DB | Single-flight + staggered TTL jitter |
| Viral post hot key | One shard overloaded | Replicate the key across shards + local LRU |

🎙️ Script: "Reads come from Redis — feeds as sorted sets, post bodies cached by id — which is how we hold sub-200ms at scale. The danger is the miss path: a viral post expiring everywhere stampedes the database. So I add single-flight, meaning only one request per key actually goes to Cassandra and the rest wait for it, plus jittered TTLs and replicating the hottest keys across shards. Worst case, a cold cache, I pre-warm and rate-limit rebuilds so we never take the DB down recovering."
🧠 If they ask "what about a Redis-wide outage?": "Feeds are rebuildable — we serve a degraded path reading recent posts from Cassandra by followee while the cache repopulates; slower, but the app stays up."

### How do we shard and replicate the databases?

**#### Great: Partition by key + replication factor 3**
🗣️ Plain words: "spread the data across many machines by an id, and keep three copies of everything so a dead machine loses nothing."
Approach: Cassandra partitions posts by postId, user-posts by authorId, follows by followeeId, each with replication factor 3 across availability zones; quorum writes for durability. Redis cluster shards feeds by userId with replicas.
Why this: even traffic distribution by key + survives node/AZ loss.
⚠️ What breaks: hot partitions — a celebrity's UserPosts partition gets hammered on reads. 
🔢 Decision-forcing math: RF=3 means a single node loss still leaves 2 copies; quorum (2 of 3) tolerates one failure with no data loss → RPO≈0 for posts.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Node dies | One replica gone | Quorum still served by other 2; repair backfills |
| Hot author partition | Read hotspot | Cache celebrity posts in Redis; replicate |
| AZ outage | A third of replicas gone | RF across 3 AZs → still quorum |

🎙️ Script: "Everything is partitioned by an access key — posts by post-id, the graph by user — and replicated three ways across availability zones with quorum writes, so one node or even one data center can fail and we lose nothing. The watch-out is hot partitions, like a celebrity's posts getting hammered, which I solve by caching those in Redis rather than letting reads hit one Cassandra partition."
🧠 If they ask "why quorum not all-replicas?": "Quorum, two of three, gives strong durability while tolerating one failure; requiring all three would mean any single node hiccup blocks writes."

### How do we handle async processing reliably + CDN/global distribution?

**#### Great: Durable queue + multi-region edge**
🗣️ Plain words: "use a log that never drops events to drive fan-out, and serve users from a data center near them."
Approach: Kafka partitioned by authorId gives ordered, durable, at-least-once delivery; consumers commit offsets after writing feeds; idempotent writes dedupe. Globally, route users to the nearest region (geo-DNS); media via CDN edge caches; feed caches regional with async cross-region replication of posts.
⚠️ What breaks: cross-region replication lag means a post might reach a far region a few seconds late (acceptable per N5); exactly-once is impractical so we use at-least-once + idempotency.
✅ Failure matrix:
| Scenario | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Consumer lag spike | Fan-out delayed | Kafka buffers durably; add consumers (scale out) |
| Region down | Users in region affected | Geo-failover to nearest region; posts replicated async |
| Duplicate delivery | Double feed write | Idempotent sorted-set write |

🎙️ Script: "Fan-out is driven off Kafka because it's a durable log — if workers fall behind, events wait safely and I just add consumers. I commit offsets only after the feed write and make the writes idempotent, so at-least-once delivery is safe. Globally, I route you to the nearest region, serve media from CDN edges, and replicate posts across regions asynchronously — a far region might be a couple seconds behind, which our eventual-consistency budget already allows."
🧠 If they ask "why not exactly-once?": "True exactly-once is expensive and brittle; at-least-once plus idempotent writes gives the same end result far more simply."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean)**
Read path target: **four nines (99.99%)** = under ~52 minutes of downtime per year. Write/post path: **99.95%** is acceptable since a brief inability to post is less catastrophic than a blank feed. Mechanisms: the read path is served from **multi-AZ** Redis (multiple data centers in one region so if one building loses power another takes over automatically) with replicas, fronted by stateless Feed Services behind a load balancer, with CDN absorbing media. No single component failure should blank the feed.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**
| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation (what the user sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feed Cache (Redis) | Pre-computed feeds unavailable | Feed loads slower; we rebuild from Cassandra by followee — user sees a slightly delayed feed, not an error | Promote replica; repopulate cache |
| Post DB (Cassandra) | Can't persist new posts / cold-cache reads fail | Posting may fail briefly (retry with idempotency key); cached feeds still serve | Quorum tolerates one node; repair |
| Kafka | Fan-out stalls | New posts are durable but reach feeds late; reads of existing feeds unaffected | Consumers resume from offsets; nothing lost |
| Fan-out Service | Feeds stop updating | Existing feeds still readable; new posts appear late | Auto-scale/restart workers; backlog drains |
| API Gateway | Total entry failure | Multiple gateway instances behind LB; one dying is invisible | LB routes around it |
| CDN | Media slow/missing | Text feed still loads; images lazy-retry | Failover origin / second CDN |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we'd lose if it crashed right now; RPO≈0 means we lose nothing because writes are replicated before we acknowledge. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until service is back after a crash.
| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Posts (source of truth) | ≈0 | < 1 min | Quorum writes RF=3 across AZs; surviving replicas serve instantly |
| Feed cache (derived) | minutes (rebuildable) | minutes | Recompute from posts + follows; no real loss |
| Follow graph | ≈0 | < 1 min | Replicated, durable |
| Media | ≈0 | seconds | S3 11-nines durability + CDN |

**9D — Cost breakdown**
Top drivers: (1) **CDN egress** — every image/video byte served to a browser is billed; at billions of media views/day this is the single biggest line item, not the database. (2) **Redis RAM** — keeping ~8TB of feeds + post bodies hot in memory across the cluster is expensive per GB. (3) **Cassandra storage + compute** for ~0.5PB replicated. Rough order-of-magnitude: low-to-mid seven figures/month at this scale. The biggest single optimization: aggressively cap feed size (~800 ids) and tune media CDN cache hit ratio + compression — a few points of CDN hit-ratio improvement dwarfs any DB saving.

🎙️ Script: "When Redis dies, you don't get an error — you get a slightly slower feed while we rebuild it from Cassandra, because the feed is derived, not truth. When Kafka backs up, your post is already safe; it just reaches followers a little late. The only thing we protect at RPO-zero is the posts and the graph themselves, via quorum replication across data centers. And the surprise on cost is that the database is cheap — it's CDN egress for media that dominates the bill."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

🔀 **Decision: Hybrid fan-out vs. pure push or pure pull**
1. What we chose and why: push for normal users (cheap reads), pull for celebrities (avoid write storms).
2. What we gave up: a uniform, simple code path — now we maintain two paths and a threshold + transition logic.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "we mail the post to most people's inboxes, but for superstars we let readers grab it on the way in — two systems instead of one."
4. When this reverses: if there are NO celebrities (flat follower graph), drop the pull path and do pure fan-out-on-write — simpler. If writes became as frequent as reads, pure pull could win.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I went hybrid because pure push dies on celebrities and pure pull dies on read volume; the cost is two code paths, which I think is worth it."

🔀 **Decision: Eventual consistency vs. strong consistency on feeds**
1. What we chose: eventual — feeds can lag a few seconds.
2. What we gave up: a guarantee that everyone sees a post the instant it's made.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "we trade 'everyone sees it this exact second' for a system that's fast and stays up."
4. When this reverses: if the product demanded real-time guarantees (e.g. a trading/auction feed), we'd need synchronous push and accept far worse write latency and cost.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Eventual consistency is the freedom that makes everything else possible — async fan-out, cheap reads. For a social feed a couple seconds late is invisible."

🔀 **Decision: Cassandra (NoSQL) vs. SQL for the core**
1. What we chose: Cassandra for posts/graph.
2. What we gave up: joins, transactions, strong consistency by default.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "we picked a database that scales writes effortlessly but can't do fancy cross-table queries."
4. When this reverses: at small scale (a few million users), a single Postgres is simpler and gives joins/transactions for free — I'd start there and migrate only when writes outgrow one primary.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Cassandra because access is by key and writes must scale linearly; if we were small I'd just use Postgres and not over-engineer."

🔀 **Decision: Async (queue-driven) vs. synchronous fan-out**
1. What we chose: async via Kafka.
2. What we gave up: immediate consistency and a simpler call stack.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: "the writer doesn't wait for the mailroom to finish delivering."
4. When this reverses: if fan-out were tiny (every user has <50 followers), synchronous inline fan-out would be simpler and fast enough.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Async because one post owes hundreds-to-millions of writes; making the author wait for that is a non-starter, and Kafka makes it durable."

🎙️ Script: "The decisions I'm least dogmatic about: hybrid fan-out — I'd collapse it to pure push if there were no celebrities; eventual consistency — the freedom I'm leaning on, which I'd give up only for a real-time product; and Cassandra — which I'd swap for Postgres at small scale. Each ties back to the same principle: reads dominate and one write owes many, so push work to write time and keep reads in memory."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two design choices: how do you actually order a feed when posts come from a push list and a celebrity pull — what's the merge?"**
The mechanism: the pushed feed is a Redis sorted set scored by timestamp; celebrity posts come from cached per-author lists also sorted by time. The Feed Service does a k-way merge by timestamp across the pushed page and the (few) celebrity lists, takes the top N, and emits a cursor of the last (ts, id).
🗣️ In plain words: "I grab my pre-sorted inbox and the latest from the few celebrities I follow, and just merge them by time like shuffling two sorted decks."
💬 One-liner: "It's a small k-way merge by timestamp — cheap because you follow only a handful of celebrities."

**❓ "What happens when Redis (the feed cache) crashes?"**
The mechanism: feeds are derived, not source of truth. We promote a replica; for any cold feed, the Feed Service rebuilds it on demand by reading the user's followees' recent posts from Cassandra (the same query the pull path uses), then backfills Redis.
🗣️ In plain words: "the inbox is just a cached copy — if it's lost we rebuild it from the original posts; the app gets slower, not broken."
💬 One-liner: "Redis is a cache, not truth — worst case the feed is slower for a bit while it rebuilds."

**❓ "It's the Super Bowl — everyone posts and refreshes at once. How do you survive the spike?"**
The mechanism: reads scale by adding stateless Feed Service instances + Redis replicas (reads come from RAM, not the DB). Writes buffer in Kafka, which absorbs the spike durably while we add fan-out consumers; the feed cap (~800) bounds work per feed. Auto-scaling triggers on queue depth and CPU.
🗣️ In plain words: "reads are served from memory so we just add machines; writes pile up safely in a queue and drain as we add workers."
💬 One-liner: "Reads scale horizontally from cache; writes are absorbed by Kafka and drained — nothing falls over, it just costs more."

**❓ "Can the same post appear twice in my feed, or get lost?"**
The mechanism: Kafka is at-least-once, so a post-id can be delivered twice — but feed writes are idempotent (sorted set keyed by post-id, so a repeat is a no-op). Loss is prevented because we commit the Kafka offset only after the feed write succeeds.
🗣️ In plain words: "duplicates collapse because the feed is keyed by post id, and we only mark an event done after it's safely written."
💬 One-liner: "At-least-once delivery plus idempotent writes means no dupes and no drops."

**❓ "How do you stop someone forging another user's post or faking a timestamp?"**
The mechanism: the author id is taken from the verified JWT, never the request body; createdAt is server-stamped. The gateway authenticates every mutating call; rate-limits curb spam. Media is uploaded via short-lived pre-signed URLs scoped to the user.
🗣️ In plain words: "we trust the login token for who you are, not anything you type in the request, and the server sets the time."
💬 One-liner: "Identity and time are server-controlled — the client can't forge who posted or when."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"**
The mechanism: CDN egress for media dominates, then Redis RAM. Cut it by tuning CDN cache hit-ratio, compressing/transcoding media, and capping feed size so RAM stays bounded; cold/inactive users' feeds can be evicted and rebuilt lazily.
🗣️ In plain words: "serving images is the big bill, not the database; better caching and compression saves the most."
💬 One-liner: "It's CDN media egress — a few points of cache hit-ratio beats any database optimization."

**❓ "How would you add likes/comments or ranked (non-chronological) feeds later?"**
The mechanism: likes/comments attach as a separate counter service (Redis counters aggregated async into Cassandra), referenced by post-id — no change to fan-out. Ranking slots in at read time: instead of returning the chronological merge directly, fetch a candidate set (the same merged ids) and score them with a feature store + model before paging.
🗣️ In plain words: "counts are a bolt-on service keyed by post; ranking just adds a scoring step right before we hand you the page."
💬 One-liner: "The chronological feed is the candidate generator — ranking and counters layer on top without touching fan-out."

**❓ "Read-your-own-writes — after I post, why do I see it instantly if fan-out is async?"**
The mechanism: the Feed Service always merges the requesting user's own recent posts (from UserPosts) into their feed page, independent of fan-out completion; the client also optimistically shows the post it just created.
🗣️ In plain words: "we always mix in your own latest posts when building your feed, so you never wait on the background delivery to see yourself."
💬 One-liner: "Your own posts are merged at read time, so you always see yourself immediately even though others see you a few seconds later."

🎙️ 60-second verbal summary: "This is a social feed where you post content and read a time-ordered stream from everyone you follow, at a scale where reads dwarf writes and one post can owe millions of feeds. The design splits into two halves. The read half is built for speed: each user's feed is pre-computed and kept in Redis as a sorted list of post-ids, served from memory in a single lookup, with post bodies cached and media on a CDN — that's how we hold p99 under 200 milliseconds at ninety thousand reads a second. The write half is built around the fan-out problem: a new post lands durably in Cassandra, emits a Kafka event, and background workers push the post-id into followers' feeds — except celebrities, whose 50-million-follower posts can't be pushed, so we skip them and merge their posts in at read time instead. The two halves connect through Kafka, the durable log that lets the author get an instant acknowledgement while delivery happens asynchronously. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: reads dominate and one write owes many, so we push work onto the write path and keep reads in memory."
